title: Streaming readers for turns.jsonl + evolution.jsonl + per-day turn-count table
depends_on: T-109 (archives exist; readers may need to look past rotated files)
current_state: agent/turn_log.py:recent_turns() and count_today() both read the entire turns.jsonl file into memory and iterate every line. evolution.py:analyze_performance() similarly loads logs/evolution.jsonl in full. At current scale (~10K turns) this is cheap. After T-109 rotation kicks in, archives are .jsonl.gz with the recent live file slim. But the streaming pattern needs to be in place BEFORE the file grows, not after; today's bug is latent.
structural_problem: Reading-the-whole-file is O(n) in file size for queries that only need the tail. The current implementation will not survive even a few months of normal logging without becoming a measurable cost.
target_state: recent_turns(n) reads ONLY the trailing portion of the file needed to gather n records (chunked tail read). count_today() reads from a per-day SQLite counter table updated by append_turn â€” O(1) instead of O(n). evolution.analyze_performance() uses the same tail-streaming pattern. All three handle rotated archives gracefully (look in archive_dir/{name}-*.jsonl.gz when recent_n records aren't in the live file).
design_notes: Tail-stream helper: _tail_jsonl(path: Path, n: int, max_bytes: int = 5_000_000) -> list[dict]. Read max_bytes from end of file, split by newline, parse last n complete lines. Drop the first partial line if file > max_bytes.
recent_turns(n): use _tail_jsonl. If fewer than n lines in live file AND archive_dir exists, walk archives newest-first decompressing as needed until n reached.
Counter table schema: CREATE TABLE turn_counts (date TEXT PRIMARY KEY, count INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0)
append_turn: after writing the jsonl line, INSERT OR REPLACE INTO turn_counts(date, count) VALUES (?, COALESCE((SELECT count FROM turn_counts WHERE date=?), 0) + 1)
count_today(): SELECT count FROM turn_counts WHERE date = today_iso â€” O(1)

Migration: on first count_today call after deploy, if turn_counts is empty, do a one-time full scan of live file to build today's counter; do not retroactively populate prior days (acceptable â€” historical counts can be reconstructed from archives if needed)
evolution.analyze_performance: similar tail-stream. If it needs full-history aggregation, accept that as a separate operation (called weekly by retro.py, can afford to be slow).
All three functions return identical output as before â€” interface-compatible, internal-only change
success_criteria: recent_turns(20) on a 1GB jsonl returns in < 100ms (benchmark)
count_today() on the same file returns in < 5ms (counter hit)
recent_turns(20) on a live file with 5 entries plus 100 in archive_dir returns 20 entries spanning both
recent_turns(20) output matches the previous implementation output on a small file (byte-for-byte equivalent dicts)
risk_notes: Counter table drift if append_turn writes succeed but counter update fails. Mitigation: catch counter failures via track_silent and tolerate small drift (count_today is a soft-stat, not authoritative). Tail-stream parse error if a record was partially written; handle by skipping unparseable lines and logging via track_silent.
migration_plan: 2. Add _open_jsonl_or_gz helper that auto-decompresses .gz files transparently
3. Rewrite recent_turns(n) to use _tail_jsonl, walking archives if needed
4. Add turn_counts.db bootstrap + counter increment in append_turn
5. Rewrite count_today to use the counter table
6. Add one-time bootstrap: if turn_counts is empty on first call, scan live file once to seed today's count
7. Mirror the tail-stream pattern in evolution.analyze_performance â€” but only for the 'recent' code path; full-history scan stays O(n) (acceptable)
8. Add testing/test_streaming_readers.py with synthetic 1GB-ish file (use sparse writes) + assertions
test_plan: test_recent_turns_tail_only â€” 100K-line file; recent_turns(20) reads only the trailing region (mock the read to assert max_bytes respected)
test_recent_turns_spans_archives â€” live file has 5; archive has 100; recent_turns(20) returns 20
test_count_today_O1 â€” append 10 turns; count_today returns 10; profiled time < 5ms
test_counter_drift_tolerated â€” patch counter update to fail; assert append_turn still succeeds (turn written, track_silent recorded)
test_tail_jsonl_handles_partial_first_line â€” file has a partial line at the chunk boundary; helper drops it cleanly
test_output_compatible_with_old_implementation â€” small file; new recent_turns output == old recent_turns output (snapshot test)
